Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''Amaterasu-Ōmikami''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:1937 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ryukyu Religion]] [[Category:Japanese Shrines]] [[Category:Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Text replacement - "completion tier" to "Completion tier]] }} [[sn:Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine]] [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[en:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[sn:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[en:Ukishima Shrine]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine}}

[[File:Yomochi_Shrine_and_Ukishima_Shrine_in_Naminoue_Shrine.JPG|Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right)|thumb]] Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine are historic Shinto sites in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, that reflect the rich cultural and spiritual traditions of the Ryukyu Islands. Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[Naminoue Shrine]] precincts . == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity =[[Amaterasu]] | Shrine Rank = | founded =1451 | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival =October 19th | Rituals = |consecrated=1451}} {{nihongo|'''Ukishima Shrine'''|浮島神社}} formerly known as {{nihongo|Chojugu Shrine|長寿宮}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} is a Shinto Shrine in Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture. It is small and located on the precincts of [[Naminoue Shrine]]. During the reign of {{ill|King Shō Kinpuku|ja|尚金福王|12=draft|12=draft}}, the construction of the {{ill|Chōkōtei|ja|長虹堤|12=draft|12=draft}} ("Long Rainbow Embankment"), which connected the previously separated islands of Naha and Shuri, faced repeated setbacks. In 1451 (Keitai 2 / Hōtoku 3), {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}} was invited from mainland Japan, and after prayers were offered, the embankment's construction was successfully completed. To commemorate this, a shrine dedicated to Amaterasu, called '''Chōju Shrine''', was established in Wakasa Town, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市|12=draft|12=draft}}. This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft|12=draft}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion tier 5]] ** [[Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|12=draft|12=draft}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|12=draft|12=draft}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[Taishō period]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude.http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''Amaterasu-Ōmikami''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:1937 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ryukyu Religion]] [[Category:Japanese Shrines]] [[Category:Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Text replacement - "completion tier" to "Completion tier]] }} [[sn:Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine]] [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[en:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[sn:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[en:Ukishima Shrine]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine}}

The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[Taishō period]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude.http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''Amaterasu-Ōmikami''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:1937 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ryukyu Religion]] [[Category:Japanese Shrines]] [[Category:Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Text replacement - "completion tier" to "Completion tier]] }} [[sn:Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine]] [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[en:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[sn:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[en:Ukishima Shrine]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Refreshing imported pages]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
[[File:Yomochi_Shrine_and_Ukishima_Shrine_in_Naminoue_Shrine.JPG|Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right)|thumb]] Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine are historic Shinto sites in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, that reflect the rich cultural and spiritual traditions of the Ryukyu Islands. Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[Naminoue Shrine]] precincts . == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity =[[Amaterasu]] | Shrine Rank = | founded =1451 | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival =October 19th | Rituals = |consecrated=1451}} {{nihongo|'''Ukishima Shrine'''|浮島神社}} formerly known as {{nihongo|Chojugu Shrine|長寿宮}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} is a Shinto Shrine in Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture. It is small and located on the precincts of [[Naminoue Shrine]]. During the reign of {{ill|King Shō Kinpuku|ja|尚金福王|12=draft|12=draft}}, the construction of the {{ill|Chōkōtei|ja|長虹堤|12=draft|12=draft}} ("Long Rainbow Embankment"), which connected the previously separated islands of Naha and Shuri, faced repeated setbacks. In 1451 (Keitai 2 / Hōtoku 3), {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=draft|12=draft}} was invited from mainland Japan, and after prayers were offered, the embankment's construction was successfully completed. To commemorate this, a shrine dedicated to Amaterasu, called '''Chōju Shrine''', was established in Wakasa Town, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市|12=draft|12=draft}}. This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft|12=draft}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion tier 5]] ** [[Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|12=draft|12=draft}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|12=draft|12=draft}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[Taishō period]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude.http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''Amaterasu-Ōmikami''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:1937 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ryukyu Religion]] [[Category:Japanese Shrines]] [[Category:Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Text replacement - "completion tier" to "Completion tier]] }} [[sn:Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine]] [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[en:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[sn:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[en:Ukishima Shrine]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine}}

However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion tier 5]] ** [[Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[Taishō period]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude.http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''Amaterasu-Ōmikami''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:1937 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ryukyu Religion]] [[Category:Japanese Shrines]] [[Category:Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Text replacement - "completion tier" to "Completion tier]] }} [[sn:Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine]] [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[en:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[sn:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[en:Ukishima Shrine]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine}}

[[File:Yomochi_Shrine_and_Ukishima_Shrine_in_Naminoue_Shrine.JPG|Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right)|thumb]] Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine are historic Shinto sites in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, that reflect the rich cultural and spiritual traditions of the Ryukyu Islands. Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[Naminoue Shrine]] precincts . == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity =[[Amaterasu]] | Shrine Rank = | founded =1451 | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival =October 19th | Rituals = |consecrated=1451}} {{nihongo|'''Ukishima Shrine'''|浮島神社}} formerly known as {{nihongo|Chojugu Shrine|長寿宮}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} is a Shinto Shrine in Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture. It is small and located on the precincts of [[Naminoue Shrine]]. During the reign of {{ill|King Shō Kinpuku|ja|尚金福王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the construction of the {{ill|Chōkōtei|ja|長虹堤|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ("Long Rainbow Embankment"), which connected the previously separated islands of Naha and Shuri, faced repeated setbacks. In 1451 (Keitai 2 / Hōtoku 3), {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was invited from mainland Japan, and after prayers were offered, the embankment's construction was successfully completed. To commemorate this, a shrine dedicated to Amaterasu, called '''Chōju Shrine''', was established in Wakasa Town, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion tier 5]] ** [[Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)

One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[Taishō period]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude.http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion tier 5]] ** [[Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[Taishō period]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude.http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''Amaterasu-Ōmikami''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:1937 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ryukyu Religion]] [[Category:Japanese Shrines]] [[Category:Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine]] [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[en:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[sn:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[en:Ukishima Shrine]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年7月9日 (日) 07:58 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 世持神社 |画像 = [[ファイル:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
波上宮境内の仮宮2社（左が世持神社、右が浮島神社） |所在地 = 沖縄県那覇市奥武山町（社殿）
沖縄県那覇市若狭1-25-11（神体） | 緯度度 = 26|緯度分 = 13 |緯度秒 = 13.9 | 経度度 = 127 |経度分 = 40|経度秒 = 15.7 |地図 = Japan Naha city |祭神 = 具志頭文若命（蔡温）
野国総管命
儀間真常命 |神体 = |社格 = 郷社 |創建 = [[1937年]] |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 11月23日 |神事 = }} '''世持神社'''（よもちじんじゃ）は、[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]][[沖縄県営奥武山公園|奥武山公園]]内に鎮座する[[神社]]（[[神社本庁]]に包括）。ただし、[[神体]]は[[社殿]]と分かれて[[波上宮]]境内に置かれている（[[#沿革|後述]]）。 == 祭神 == 琉球に繁栄をもたらした恩人として、以下の3人が祀られている。 * [[蔡温]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html 世持神社] - 沖縄県神社庁、2017年7月25日閲覧。 * [[野國總管|野国総管]] * [[儀間真常]] == 沿革 == 世持神社は、[[国場川]]にかつてあった中州島（奥武山）の森に、1937年（[[昭和]]12年）に創建された。社名の「世持」は「豊かなる御世、平和なる御世を支え持つ」を意味する沖縄古語である。[[1939年]]（昭和14年）には沖縄県内唯一の郷社（当時）に列せられ、世直しと殖産振興の神社として県内の砂糖業界・農林業界・教育界から崇敬を受けたが、社殿は境内の森と共に[[沖縄戦]]により完全に破壊された。 戦火を免れた世持神社の神体は、戦後、沖縄総鎮守の波上宮に預けられたが、すでに那覇市は奥武山公園内に社を再建することを決めており、社殿は奥武山、神体は波上宮に分かれる形となった。 現在、波上宮境内には浮島神社の仮社殿と並んで世持神社の仮社殿がある。 === 建立までの経緯 === 琉球[[サツマイモ|甘藷]]栽培の功労者[[野國總管]]らを祀る世持神社は建立までに紆余曲折を辿っている。野國の偉業を評し彼の子孫は琉球士族に列せられ、その後の琉球近世に野國の顕彰碑が建てられる。 野國の子孫らは[[儀間真常]]麻氏門中を巻き込み、沖縄の農業団体の多くをあげて沖縄県や時の内務省に再三再四の陳情を行い、ようやく[[近代社格制度|郷社]]としての建立に至ったと言う経緯がある。なお却下のあった理由の一つに、野國や儀間だけが祭神では[[位階]]が低いとの理由があった。このため[[蔡氏儀間殿内]]分家の子孫で琉球[[三司官]]を務めた[[蔡温]]を列に加えた。さらに、[[大正天皇]]崩御による蔡温への位階追贈（[[正五位]]）が決め手になったと言う。 沖縄戦と、それ以降の米軍進出による施設整備の中で、かつてあった世持神社社殿は奥武山の森と共に完全に消滅した。 == 例大祭 == 例大祭は11月23日で、奥武山公園内の社殿で波上宮神職によって執り行われる。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[沖縄県の神社一覧]] == 外部リンク == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html 世持神社] - 沖縄県神社庁 {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:よもちしんしや}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧郷社]] [[Category:那覇市の建築物]] [[Category:那覇市の歴史]] [[Category:1937年開業の施設]]

儀間真常命 |神体 = |社格 = 郷社 |創建 = [[1937年]] |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 11月23日 |神事 = }} '''世持神社'''（よもちじんじゃ）は、[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]][[沖縄県営奥武山公園|奥武山公園]]内に鎮座する[[神社]]（[[神社本庁]]に包括）。ただし、[[神体]]は[[社殿]]と分かれて[[波上宮]]境内に置かれている（[[#沿革|後述]]）。 == 祭神 == 琉球に繁栄をもたらした恩人として、以下の3人が祀られている。 * [[蔡温]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html 世持神社] - 沖縄県神社庁、2017年7月25日閲覧。 * [[野國總管|野国総管]] * [[儀間真常]] == 沿革 == 世持神社は、[[国場川]]にかつてあった中州島（奥武山）の森に、1937年（[[昭和]]12年）に創建された。社名の「世持」は「豊かなる御世、平和なる御世を支え持つ」を意味する沖縄古語である。[[1939年]]（昭和14年）には沖縄県内唯一の郷社（当時）に列せられ、世直しと殖産振興の神社として県内の砂糖業界・農林業界・教育界から崇敬を受けたが、社殿は境内の森と共に[[沖縄戦]]により完全に破壊された。 戦火を免れた世持神社の神体は、戦後、沖縄総鎮守の波上宮に預けられたが、すでに那覇市は奥武山公園内に社を再建することを決めており、社殿は奥武山、神体は波上宮に分かれる形となった。 現在、波上宮境内には浮島神社の仮社殿と並んで世持神社の仮社殿がある。 === 建立までの経緯 === 琉球[[サツマイモ|甘藷]]栽培の功労者[[野國總管]]らを祀る世持神社は建立までに紆余曲折を辿っている。野國の偉業を評し彼の子孫は琉球士族に列せられ、その後の琉球近世に野國の顕彰碑が建てられる。 野國の子孫らは[[儀間真常]]麻氏門中を巻き込み、沖縄の農業団体の多くをあげて沖縄県や時の内務省に再三再四の陳情を行い、ようやく[[近代社格制度|郷社]]としての建立に至ったと言う経緯がある。なお却下のあった理由の一つに、野國や儀間だけが祭神では[[位階]]が低いとの理由があった。このため[[蔡氏儀間殿内]]分家の子孫で琉球[[三司官]]を務めた[[蔡温]]を列に加えた。さらに、[[大正天皇]]崩御による蔡温への位階追贈（[[正五位]]）が決め手になったと言う。 沖縄戦と、それ以降の米軍進出による施設整備の中で、かつてあった世持神社社殿は奥武山の森と共に完全に消滅した。 == 例大祭 == 例大祭は11月23日で、奥武山公園内の社殿で波上宮神職によって執り行われる。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[沖縄県の神社一覧]] == 外部リンク == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html 世持神社] - 沖縄県神社庁 {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:よもちしんしや}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧郷社]] [[Category:那覇市の建築物]] [[Category:那覇市の歴史]] [[Category:1937年開業の施設]]

[[File:Yomochi_Shrine_and_Ukishima_Shrine_in_Naminoue_Shrine.JPG|Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right)|thumb]] Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine are historic Shinto sites in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, that reflect the rich cultural and spiritual traditions of the Ryukyu Islands. Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[Naminoue Shrine]] precincts . == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity =[[Amaterasu]] | Shrine Rank = | founded =1451 | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival =October 19th | Rituals = |consecrated=1451}} {{nihongo|'''Ukishima Shrine'''|浮島神社}} formerly known as {{nihongo|Chojugu Shrine|長寿宮}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} is a Shinto Shrine in Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture. It is small and located on the precincts of [[Naminoue Shrine]]. During the reign of {{ill|King Shō Kinpuku|ja|尚金福王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the construction of the {{ill|Chōkōtei|ja|長虹堤|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ("Long Rainbow Embankment"), which connected the previously separated islands of Naha and Shuri, faced repeated setbacks. In 1451 (Keitai 2 / Hōtoku 3), {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was invited from mainland Japan, and after prayers were offered, the embankment's construction was successfully completed. To commemorate this, a shrine dedicated to Amaterasu, called '''Chōju Shrine''', was established in Wakasa Town, {{ill|Naha|ja|那覇市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion tier 5]] ** [[Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[Taishō period]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{citation needed|Ukishima Shrine}} {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude.http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''Amaterasu-Ōmikami''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Former village shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:1937 establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ryukyu Religion]] [[Category:Japanese Shrines]] [[Category:Ukishima Shrine]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Ukishima Shrine and Yomochi Shrine]] [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[en:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[sn:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine]] [[en:Ukishima Shrine]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine}} {{translated page|ja|世持神社|version=84022145|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[File:Yomochi_Shrine_and_Ukishima_Shrine_in_Naminoue_Shrine.JPG|Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right)|thumb]] Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine are historic Shinto sites in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, that reflect the rich cultural and spiritual traditions of the Ryukyu Islands. Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[Naminoue Shrine]] precincts . == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
