This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude. However, after the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]]''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] ** [[:en:Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[:en:Kokuba River|Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} ** }} {{translated page|ja|世持神社|version=84022145|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1937]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{wikidata link|Q38278417}}

[[ja:世持神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q38278417]] Wikidata name: Yomochi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yomochi_Shrine_and_Ukishima_Shrine_in_Naminoue_Shrine.JPG|Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right)|thumb]] Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines are historic Shinto sites in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, that reflect the rich cultural and spiritual traditions of the Ryukyu Islands. Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] precincts http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html. == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity ={{ill|Gima Shinjō|WD=Q4699378}}; {{ill|Noguni Sōkan|WD=Q4699326}}; {{ill|Sai On|WD=Q851766}} | Shrine Rank = | founded =1451 | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival =October 19th | Rituals = |consecrated=1451| location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.220528|N|127.671028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ukishima Shrine'''|浮島神社}} formerly known as {{nihongo|Chojugu Shrine|長寿宮}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} is a Shinto Shrine in Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture. It is small and located on the precincts of [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]]. During the reign of [[:en:Shō Kinpuku|King Shō Kinpuku]], the construction of the {{ill|Chōkōtei|ja|長虹堤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōtei|qq=|1=Chōkōtei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653653}} ("Long Rainbow Embankment"), which connected the previously separated islands of Naha and Shuri, faced repeated setbacks. In 1451 (Keitai 2 / Hōtoku 3), [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] was invited from mainland Japan, and after prayers were offered, the embankment's construction was successfully completed. To commemorate this, a shrine dedicated to Amaterasu, called '''Chōju Shrine''', was established in Wakasa Town, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]. This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude. However, after the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]]''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] ** [[:en:Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]

It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|qq=|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[:en:Kokuba River|Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} ** }} {{translated page|ja|世持神社|version=84022145|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1937]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{wikidata link|Q38278417}}

[[ja:世持神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q38278417]] Wikidata name: Yomochi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yomochi_Shrine_and_Ukishima_Shrine_in_Naminoue_Shrine.JPG|Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right)|thumb]] Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines are historic Shinto sites in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, that reflect the rich cultural and spiritual traditions of the Ryukyu Islands. Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] precincts http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html. == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity ={{ill|Gima Shinjō|WD=Q4699378}}; {{ill|Noguni Sōkan|WD=Q4699326}}; {{ill|Sai On|WD=Q851766}} | Shrine Rank = | founded =1451 | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival =October 19th | Rituals = |consecrated=1451| location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.220528|N|127.671028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ukishima Shrine'''|浮島神社}} formerly known as {{nihongo|Chojugu Shrine|長寿宮}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} is a Shinto Shrine in Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture. It is small and located on the precincts of [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]]. During the reign of [[:en:Shō Kinpuku|King Shō Kinpuku]], the construction of the {{ill|Chōkōtei|ja|長虹堤|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōtei|1=Chōkōtei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653653}} ("Long Rainbow Embankment"), which connected the previously separated islands of Naha and Shuri, faced repeated setbacks. In 1451 (Keitai 2 / Hōtoku 3), [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] was invited from mainland Japan, and after prayers were offered, the embankment's construction was successfully completed. To commemorate this, a shrine dedicated to Amaterasu, called '''Chōju Shrine''', was established in Wakasa Town, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]. This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude. However, after the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]]''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] ** [[:en:Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[:en:Kokuba River|Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} ** }} {{translated page|ja|世持神社|version=84022145|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1937]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{wikidata link|Q38278417}}

This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude. However, after the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]]''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] ** [[:en:Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[:en:Kokuba River|Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} ** }} {{translated page|ja|世持神社|version=84022145|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1937]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{wikidata link|Q38278417}}

[[ja:世持神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q38278417]] Wikidata name: Yomochi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yomochi_Shrine_and_Ukishima_Shrine_in_Naminoue_Shrine.JPG|Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right)|thumb]] Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines are historic Shinto sites in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, that reflect the rich cultural and spiritual traditions of the Ryukyu Islands. Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] precincts http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html. == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity ={{ill|Gima Shinjō|WD=Q4699378}}; {{ill|Noguni Sōkan|WD=Q4699326}}; {{ill|Sai On|WD=Q851766}} | Shrine Rank = | founded =1451 | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival =October 19th | Rituals = |consecrated=1451| location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.220528|N|127.671028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ukishima Shrine'''|浮島神社}} formerly known as {{nihongo|Chojugu Shrine|長寿宮}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} is a Shinto Shrine in Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture. It is small and located on the precincts of [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]]. During the reign of [[:en:Shō Kinpuku|King Shō Kinpuku]], the construction of the {{ill|Chōkōtei|ja|長虹堤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōtei|1=Chōkōtei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653653}} ("Long Rainbow Embankment"), which connected the previously separated islands of Naha and Shuri, faced repeated setbacks. In 1451 (Keitai 2 / Hōtoku 3), [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] was invited from mainland Japan, and after prayers were offered, the embankment's construction was successfully completed. To commemorate this, a shrine dedicated to Amaterasu, called '''Chōju Shrine''', was established in Wakasa Town, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]. This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude. However, after the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]]''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] ** [[:en:Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[:en:Kokuba River|Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} ** }} {{translated page|ja|世持神社|version=84022145|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1937]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{wikidata link|Q38278417}}

Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] precincts http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html. == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity ={{ill|Gima Shinjō|WD=Q4699378}}; {{ill|Noguni Sōkan|WD=Q4699326}}; {{ill|Sai On|WD=Q851766}} | Shrine Rank = | founded =1451 | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival =October 19th | Rituals = |consecrated=1451| location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.220528|N|127.671028|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} {{nihongo|'''Ukishima Shrine'''|浮島神社}} formerly known as {{nihongo|Chojugu Shrine|長寿宮}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} is a Shinto Shrine in Naha City, Okinawa Prefecture. It is small and located on the precincts of [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]]. During the reign of [[:en:Shō Kinpuku|King Shō Kinpuku]], the construction of the {{ill|Chōkōtei|ja|長虹堤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōkōtei|1=Chōkōtei|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11653653}} ("Long Rainbow Embankment"), which connected the previously separated islands of Naha and Shuri, faced repeated setbacks. In 1451 (Keitai 2 / Hōtoku 3), [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] was invited from mainland Japan, and after prayers were offered, the embankment's construction was successfully completed. To commemorate this, a shrine dedicated to Amaterasu, called '''Chōju Shrine''', was established in Wakasa Town, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]. This shrine later became known as Ukishima Shrine and was relocated to the temporary shrine at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] in 1988 (Shōwa 63). Chōju Shrine is historically recognized as the first shrine constructed in the Ryukyu Islands documented in historical records. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 ==== History ==== Ukishima Shrine, originally known as Chōju-gū, has a storied history dating back to 1451 during the reign of King Shō Kinpuku. According to historical records such as "Kyūyō" and other traditional accounts, the national minister Kaiki undertook the construction of a long embankment known as Chōkōtei. The construction faced significant challenges due to the depth and turbulence of the sea. To overcome these difficulties, Kaiki established an altar and engaged in continuous prayers over two nights and three days. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude. However, after the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942. It suffered destruction during the war in 1944 but was reestablished in Naha's Ameku district in October 1965. Subsequently, on July 25, 1988, the shrine was relocated to its current temporary site within Naminoue Shrine. ==== Enshrined Deities ==== * '''[[Amaterasu|Amaterasu-Ōmikami]]''': The principal deity worshipped at Ukishima Shrine. * '''Associated Deities''': ** [[:en:Toyouke-hime|Toyouke-Ōmikami]] ** [[:en:Ame-no-Tajikarao|Ame-no-Tajikarao-no-Kami]] ** [[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi-no-Kami]] ** [[Sukunabikona|Sukunahikona-no-Kami]] ** [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi-no-Kami]] == Yomochi Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Yomochi Shrine | image = [[File:Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines in Naminoue Shrine.JPG|250px]]
Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right) | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)
Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Wakasa 1-25-11 (shrine deity) | type degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 13.9 | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 15.7 | map = Japan Naha city | deity = * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]][http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017. * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} | Shrine Rank = Gōsha (village shrine) | founded = [[1937]] | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = | festival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Yomochi Shrine''' (世持神社, Yomochi Jinja) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within [[Okinawa Prefectural Ounoyama Park]] in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. However, the shrine deity is enshrined separately at [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] (details below). === Deities === The shrine honors three individuals who significantly contributed to the prosperity of the Ryukyu Kingdom: * [[:en:Sai On|Sai On]] * {{ill|8=ja|9=野國總管|Noguni Sōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Noguni Sōkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699326}} * {{ill|8=ja|9=儀間真常|Gima Shinjō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/8=ja|1=Gima Shinjō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4699378}} === History === Yomochi Shrine was established in 1937 (Showa 12) in the forest on a former island (Ounoyama) in the [[:en:Kokuba River|Kokuba River]]. The name "Yomochi" means "supporting a rich and peaceful reign" in ancient Okinawan language. In 1939 (Showa 14), it was the only shrine in Okinawa to be designated as a Gōsha (village shrine), revered by the sugar, agriculture, forestry, and education sectors for promoting prosperity and well-being. However, the shrine building and the surrounding forest were completely destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. The shrine deity, which survived the war, was entrusted to Naminoue Shrine. Despite plans by the Naha City to rebuild the shrine in Ounoyama Park, the shrine deity remains separated from the main shrine building, which is located in Ounoyama, while the deity is housed in Naminoue Shrine. Currently, a temporary shrine for Yomochi Shrine stands within the Naminoue Shrine precincts alongside a temporary shrine for Ukishima Shrine. ==== Establishment Process ==== The establishment of Yomochi Shrine, dedicated to figures like Nokuni Sokan for his contributions to sweet potato cultivation in the Ryukyu Kingdom, underwent a long and challenging process. The descendants of Nokuni were elevated to the Ryukyu nobility, and a monument was erected in his honor in later centuries. The descendants of Nokuni, alongside the Gima Shōjō lineage, involved various Okinawan agricultural organizations in persistent petitions to the Okinawan government and the Ministry of Home Affairs, leading to its eventual establishment as a Gōsha. One reason for initial rejections was the perceived low Rank of enshrining only Nokuni and Gima. Thus, Sai On, a prominent Ryukyu official from the Sai family, was added to the list of deities. The posthumous conferment of Rank on Sai On during the [[:en:Taishō era|Taishō era]] played a decisive role in the shrine's establishment. The original shrine building, along with the Ounoyama forest, was completely obliterated during the war and subsequent U.S. military expansions. === festival === The festival is held on November 23, conducted by the priests of Naminoue Shrine at the shrine building within Ounoyama Park. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Pages == * [[List of Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == External Links == *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office ==References== {{Kumano Faith}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} ** }} {{translated page|ja|世持神社|version=84022145|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: [[ja:世持神社]] [[ja:浮島神社]] [[ja:%E6%B5%AE%E5%B6%8B%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE (%E9%82%A3%E8%A6%87%E5%B8%82)]] [[ja:&action=edit&redlink=1]] [[Category:Facilities Opened in 1937]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{wikidata link|Q38278417}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[ja:世持神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q38278417]] Wikidata name: Yomochi Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Yomochi_Shrine_and_Ukishima_Shrine_in_Naminoue_Shrine.JPG|Temporary shrines within Naminoue Shrine precincts (Yomochi Shrine on the left, Ukishima Shrine on the right)|thumb]] Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines are historic Shinto sites in Naha, Okinawa Prefecture, Japan, that reflect the rich cultural and spiritual traditions of the Ryukyu Islands. Established in 1937, Yomochi Shrine honors key figures of the Ryukyu Kingdom—such as Sai On, Noguni Sōkan, and Gima Shinjō—and, despite its wartime destruction, continues its legacy through the enshrinement of its deity at Naminoue Shrine [http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/yomochi.html Yomochi Shrine] - Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Office, accessed July 25, 2017.. In contrast, Ukishima Shrine, originally founded in 1451 as Chojugu Shrine to commemorate the divine intervention of Amaterasu-Ōmikami during the construction of a critical embankment, is recognized as the earliest documented shrine in the region; after suffering war damage and subsequent relocations, it now exists as a temporary shrine within the [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]] precincts http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/ukishima.html. == Ukishima Shrine == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| name = Ukishima Shrine | image = File:Yomochi Shrine and Ukishima Shrine in Naminoue Shrine.JPG | location = Okinawa Prefecture, Naha City, Ounoyama-cho (shrine building)

In 1451 (Keitai 2 / Hōtoku 3), [[:en:Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]] was invited from mainland Japan, and after prayers were offered, the embankment's construction was successfully completed. To commemorate this, a shrine dedicated to Amaterasu, called '''Chōju Shrine''', was established in Wakasa Town, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]. It is excluded from {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} due to its lack of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}} Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。 The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。 In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. Miraculously, the seawater receded, revealing the seabed, which allowed the Completion of the embankment from Asato Bridge to Ibegaama. This divine intervention was attributed to the blessings of the deity, prompting the establishment of a shrine dedicated to the Sun Goddess, Amaterasu-Ōmikami, and named Chōju-gū in gratitude. However, after the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} The shrine was later renamed Ukishima Shrine in 1942.
